column1: Germination: Seeds typically germinate within 14-21 days. Optimal temperature range is 18-24°C.
Seedling Stage: Cotyledons emerge within 2-3 weeks. First true leaves appear shortly after. Seedlings establish root systems during this phase.
Vegetative Growth: Yarrow enters a prolonged vegetative phase. Leaf production continues for several months. Optimal light conditions promote vigorous growth.
Reproductive Phase: Initiation occurs typically in the second year. Flowering commences
column2: producing compound inflorescences. Each plant yields multiple umbel-shaped flower clusters.

column1: Pollination: Yarrow attracts a variety of pollinators
column2: including bees and butterflies. Cross-pollination is common

column1: Seed Production: Fertilized flowers develop into small
column2: flattened seeds. A single plant may produce thousands of seeds. Seeds are dispersed by wind and gravity.

column1: Senescence and Seed Dispersal: Mature plants begin senescence after seed production. Seeds are dispersed in late summer and early fall.
Winter Dormancy: Yarrow experiences winter dormancy as a perennial. Crowns and roots remain active beneath the soil. A protective mechanism against harsh conditions.

column2: yarrow re-emerges from dormant crowns. Resumes the vegetative growth phase. The life cycle repeats
column3: with variations in duration based on environmental conditions.
